system_message:
# Scripture Study — System Context

You are a scripture study assistant collaborating with a human who brings faith in
Jesus Christ and the Restoration. Your role is to facilitate deep, honest scripture
study — producing not just knowledge but personal transformation.

## Core Values

- **Depth over breadth.** Take time to really explore. Surface patterns that casual
  reading misses. Trace words to Hebrew/Greek. Compare across all five standard works.
  Follow the footnotes.
- **Honest exploration.** Engage thoughtfully with hard questions rather than retreating
  to disclaimers. Uncertainty can be acknowledged warmly.
- **Faith as framework.** Work within the framework of faith in Jesus Christ and the
  Restoration. Offer scholarly insight AND spiritual application.

## Constraints

- Every direct quote must be accurate. Do not fabricate or approximate quotations.
- Link claims to specific scriptures. Chapter and verse, not vague references.
- When you don't know something, say so. A faithful "I'm not sure" is better than
  a confident fabrication.

## What Good Output Looks Like

- Cross-references that surface unexpected connections between standard works
- Webster 1828 word analysis that illuminates Restoration-era vocabulary
- Teaching patterns extracted from conference talks
- Doctrinal synthesis that traces themes across multiple sources
- Specific, actionable insights — not generic spiritual platitudes

## Standard Works

The five standard works are: Old Testament, New Testament, Book of Mormon,
Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price. Conference talks from
General Conference (1971–present) are also available.


# Gospel Vocabulary for LLM Context

*Context engineering document — curated theological patterns for conference reindex scoring*

---

## Purpose

This document gives you the theological vocabulary to recognize Christ-connections that exist below the surface of scriptural text. It is NOT a comprehensive theology. It is 7 curated patterns, each grounded in specific verses, that enable accurate scoring of typological depth.

**Critical constraint:** These patterns help you RECOGNIZE what's already in the text — not project Christ-connections onto text where they don't exist. A passage about agricultural practice is not Christological because seeds are mentioned. A talk about organizational logistics is not about the doctrine of Christ because the organization teaches it. Look for *textual evidence*: explicit identification, verbal echoes of scriptural language, prophetic commentary linking symbol to Christ, footnotes, cross-references. Surface keyword matches without theological reasoning do not count.

---

## Pattern 1: The Doctrine of Christ

The gospel has a defined core. Christ himself declared it three times in a single sermon:

> "This is my doctrine... the Father commandeth all men, everywhere, to repent and believe in me." "Whoso believeth in me, and is baptized, the same shall be saved." "Whoso buildeth upon this buildeth upon my rock, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against them." (3 Ne 11:32-33, 39)

Christ's second definition adds the narrative arc: "I came into the world to do the will of my Father... that I might be lifted up upon the cross... that I might draw all men unto me" (3 Ne 27:13-14). Here the gospel is not just steps but Christ's entire mission and atonement.

Nephi adds the experiential dimension: repentance + baptism = the gate; then "press forward with a steadfastness in Christ, having a perfect brightness of hope, and a love of God and of all men... feasting upon the word of Christ, and endure to the end... Ye shall have eternal life" (2 Ne 31:17-20). The Articles of Faith state it most compactly: "Faith in the Lord Jesus Christ; Repentance; Baptism by immersion for the remission of sins; Laying on of hands for the gift of the Holy Ghost" (AofF 1:4).

When content develops what these elements mean for the learner — not just mentions them — it is engaging the doctrine of Christ.

---

## Pattern 2: Tree of Life = Love of God = Christ

Nephi's vision (1 Ne 11) provides the interpretive key for all tree, seed, and fruit imagery in the Book of Mormon. The angel shows Nephi Christ's birth, ministry, and crucifixion, then asks the meaning of the tree. Nephi answers:

> "The love of God, which sheddeth itself abroad in the hearts of the children of men; wherefore, it is the most desirable above all things... the most joyous to the soul." (1 Ne 11:22-23)

The vision explicitly identifies: rod of iron = word of God, waters = love of God, tree of life = love of God (1 Ne 11:25). The condescension of God — Christ's life and death — IS the meaning of the tree.

Alma 32's seed parable connects directly. The word planted becomes "a tree, springing up in you unto everlasting life" (Alma 33:23), bearing fruit "most precious... sweet above all that is sweet, white above all that is white" (Alma 32:42) — verbatim echoes of Lehi's description of the fruit (1 Ne 8:11). The seed parable is the tree of life vision restated in instructional form.

---

## Pattern 3: The Word Is Christ

"The word" in scripture carries a dual meaning: the word of God (the message, the gospel) and the Word (Christ himself). John establishes the identification: "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God... And the Word was made flesh" (John 1:1, 14).

This duality means planting "the word" in your heart (Alma 32:28) is simultaneously receiving the gospel AND receiving Christ. Alma 33:22-23 resolves any ambiguity: "begin to believe in the Son of God... plant this word in your hearts... through the joy of his Son."

When a passage talks about receiving, planting, nourishing, or holding to "the word," it may be operating on both levels — the message AND the person.

---

## Pattern 4: "All Things Testify of Christ"

God told Adam directly: "All things have their likeness, and all things are created and made to bear record of me, both things which are temporal, and things which are spiritual... all things bear record of me" (Moses 6:63). Nephi adds: "all things which have been given of God from the beginning of the world, unto man, are the typifying of him" (2 Ne 11:4).

This is the typological principle: even passages that don't name Christ may encode him through types, symbols, or structural parallels.

**However:** this principle requires specific evidence — a textual connection, a footnote, a prophetic interpretation, a verbal echo. The fact that "all things" testify does not mean every talk mentioning any positive theme is teaching about Christ. Pattern 4 says types EXIST; it does not say everything encountered IS a type. Look for the specific textual evidence that links a symbol to Christ.

---

## Pattern 5: Faith, Hope, and Charity

The character trajectory of a disciple: faith precedes hope (Moro 7:42), hope comes "through the atonement of Christ" (Moro 7:41), and charity — "the pure love of Christ" — "endureth forever" (Moro 7:47). The chain runs through meekness and the Holy Ghost: "if a man be meek and lowly in heart, and confesses by the power of the Holy Ghost that Jesus is the Christ, he must needs have charity" (Moro 7:44).

Moroni prays "that ye may be filled with this love... that ye may become the sons of God... that we shall be like him" (Moro 7:48). Charity is not behavior alone — it is becoming like Christ. And Ether 12:34 identifies Christ's own love for the world as charity: "this love which thou hast had for the children of men is charity."

Paul establishes the hierarchy: "now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three; but the greatest of these is charity" (1 Cor 13:13). The Greek footnote confirms charity = agape = love.

---

## Pattern 6: "Love Shed Abroad"

A theological thread connecting the tree of life to the Holy Ghost to diligent discipleship:

- **What it is:** "the love of God, which sheddeth itself abroad in the hearts of the children of men" (1 Ne 11:22)
- **How it arrives:** "the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost which is given unto us" (Rom 5:5)
- **What sustains it:** the Comforter "filleth with hope and perfect love, which love endureth by diligence unto prayer" (Moro 8:26)

Three independent witnesses — Nephi, Paul, Mormon — using the same language for the same concept. The mechanism: God's love enters through the Holy Ghost and is sustained by active practice. Mormon gives the fullest chain: repentance → baptism → commandments → remission of sins → meekness → Holy Ghost → "hope and perfect love" → diligence → dwell with God (Moro 8:25-26). This is the operational bridge between the doctrine of Christ (Pattern 1) and the fruit of the tree (Pattern 2).

---

## Pattern 7: Types and Shadows

Specific symbolic patterns that encode Christ across dispensations:

- **Brass serpent:** "a type was raised up in the wilderness, that whosoever would look upon it might live" (Alma 33:19). Christ identifies himself with it: "as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, even so must the Son of man be lifted up" (John 3:14).
- **Paschal lamb:** "Christ our passover is sacrificed for us" (1 Cor 5:7).
- **Living water:** "the water that I shall give him shall be in him a well of water springing up into everlasting life" (John 4:14). Nephi identifies the fountain of living waters as "a representation of the love of God" (1 Ne 11:25).
- **Bread of life:** "I am the bread of life: he that cometh to me shall never hunger; and he that believeth on me shall never thirst" (John 6:35).

These types are explicitly identified as Christ in scripture. When a passage references them, it is engaging a Christ-type — even if Christ is not named.

---

## How These Connect

These 7 patterns are not independent categories. They form one theological architecture:

The **tree of life** (Pattern 2) IS the **love of God**, which IS **charity** (Pattern 5, Ether 12:34). That love enters hearts through the **Holy Ghost** (Pattern 6), which is received through the **doctrine of Christ** (Pattern 1). The **word** planted in the heart (Pattern 3) IS Christ himself, growing into the tree. The **types** (Pattern 7) — bread, water, serpent, lamb — all point to the same reality: Christ as the source of life. And **all things testify** of this (Pattern 4) — but only when the textual evidence supports the connection.

Recognizing this architecture helps you see how a talk that develops one pattern deeply may implicitly engage others. But each pattern should be scored based on what the text explicitly develops, not on assumed connections.
response:
KEYWORDS: [Jesus Christ, Book of Mormon, restoration, love, testimony, miracles, doctrine of Christ, Holy Ghost, redemption, covenant, discipleship, scripture, witness, grace, salvation]  
SUMMARY: Elder Jeffrey R. Holland teaches that the gospel of Christ is revealed through simple, divine means that bring miraculous transformation. He uses the biblical account of Jesus anointing the blind man’s eyes to illustrate how ordinary instruments can manifest God’s power, and he testifies that the Book of Mormon is another witness of Christ that deepens love, faith, and personal conversion.  
KEY_QUOTE: "Amazing grace—how sweet the sound—That saved a wretch like me!"  
TEACHING_PROFILE:

MODE: declared (testifies)
PATTERN: story→doctrine→invitation
TEACH_SCORE: 8
HELP_SCORE: 8
LOVE_SCORE: 9
SPIRIT_SCORE: 8
DOCTRINE_SCORE: 7
INVITE_SCORE: 6
user_message: /no_think Create a summary and teaching profile of this conference talk. FORMAT — output EXACTLY these sections: KEYWORDS: [10-15 comma-separated terms: doctrines, people, events, themes] SUMMARY: [50-75 word narrative covering main message and teachings, present tense] KEY_QUOTE: [Most memorable or powerful direct quote from the talk] TEACHING_PROFILE: DOMINANT: [1-2 most prominent from: teach_about_christ, help_come_to_christ, love, spirit, doctrine, invite] MODE: [primary from: enacted (models it) | declared (testifies) | doctrinal (explains) | experiential (shares experience)] PATTERN: [brief label: "story→doctrine→invitation" or "problem→principle→promise"] TEACH_SCORE: [0-9, how central is Christ to the content] HELP_SCORE: [0-9, how much does this help people come to Christ] LOVE_SCORE: [0-9, how strongly does this demonstrate or teach love] SPIRIT_SCORE: [0-9, how directly does this invite the Spirit or bear testimony] DOCTRINE_SCORE: [0-9, how deeply does this engage with doctrine] INVITE_SCORE: [0-9, how directly does this invite to specific action] Scoring guidance: - 3 = present but not central - 5 = a clear theme, well-developed - 7 = a defining feature, specific memorable teaching - 9 = rare — redefines understanding of this dimension # And Now I See 🎧 [Listen to Audio](https://assets.churchofjesuschrist.org/q57j423iko0mmmzivy5ibhcydx1s2pz3hozycn4r-128k-en.mp3) # And Now I See By Elder Jeffrey R. Holland Of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles The impact of the Book of Mormon in my life is no less miraculous than was the application of spit and dirt placed on the blind man’s eyes. With love unfeigned we all echo President Oaks’s tribute to the passing of President Russell M. Nelson. And with equal love and deep mourning, we all acknowledge the tragedies in Michigan recently and almost daily around the world. We acknowledge these things with love and trust in the Lord Jesus Christ. The [ninth chapter of John](../../../scriptures/nt/john/9.md) records the experience of Jesus and His disciples passing near a beggar, blind from birth. This led the disciples to ask Jesus several complex religious questions regarding the origin and transmission of this man’s limitation. The Master responded by doing something very simple and very surprising. He spit into the dirt and stirred a small mixture of clay. He then applied this to the eyes of the man, instructing him to wash in the pool of Siloam. All this the sightless man obediently did and “came \[forth] seeing,” the scripture says.[1](#fn-1) How important evidence is, as opposed to wishes or argument or even malice in opposition to the truth. Well, afraid this miracle would again add to the threat Jesus already posed to their presumed authority, the enemies of the Savior confronted the newly sighted man and said in anger, “We know \[Jesus] is a sinner.” The man listened for a moment, then said, “Whether he be a sinner … , I know not: \[but] one thing I \[do] know, … whereas I was blind, now I see.”[2](#fn-2) Jesus gave the first meaning to this exchange, telling His disciples that all this had happened “that the works of God should be made manifest.”[3](#fn-3) Remember that twice in this narrative the Savior’s action was referred to as “anointing”[4](#fn-4) the blind man’s eyes, an act to be completed by washing. This description of “the works of God \[being] made manifest”[5](#fn-5) could possibly suggest the unfolding of an ordinance. Another truth that is evident here are the instruments the Creator of heaven and earth and all that in them are used to provide this miracle: spit and a handful of dirt! These very unlikely ingredients declare that God can bless us by whatever method He chooses. Like Naaman resisting the River Jordan[6](#fn-6) or the children of Israel refusing to look at the serpent on the staff,[7](#fn-7) how easy it is for us to dismiss the source of our redemption because the ingredients and the instruments seem embarrassingly plain. But we remember from the Book of Mormon that some things are both plain *and precious* and that prior to Jesus’s birth, it would be prophesied that “he \[would have] no form nor comeliness; and when we \[should] see him, there is no beauty that we should desire him.”[8](#fn-8) How often God has sent His majestic message through a newly called and very anxious Relief Society president or an unlearned boy on a New York farm or a brand-new missionary or a baby lying in a manger. So what if the answers to our prayers come in plain or convoluted ways? Are we willing to persevere, to keep trying to live Christ’s gospel no matter how much spit and clay it takes? It may not always be clear to us what is being done or why, and from time to time, we will all feel a little like the senior sister who said, “Lord, how about a blessing that isn’t in disguise?” Consider the evidence of another truth, this one regarding the holy priesthood. In documenting the organization of the meridian Church, Luke’s first line reads, “Then he called his twelve disciples together, and gave them power and authority,”[9](#fn-9) gifts not granted on the basis of impressive credentials nor determined by tradition or birthright. They are not bestowed by a divinity school or a theological seminary. They are conferred only by the laying on of hands by one who has had authorized hands laid on him in an unbroken sequence back to the source of all divine authority, the Lord Jesus Christ. And in a church that understands the gift of mercy, wouldn’t it be another marvelous evidence of that church’s truthfulness to see these blessings and covenants go to our deceased kindred, those of our families who have gone before us? Should they be penalized because they did not have access to the gospel or because they were born at a time or in a place when divine ordinances and covenants were not available to them? The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has sacred, dedicated houses of the Lord in which merciful, salvific work is being done vicariously every day and night for these deceased, as well as offering worship opportunities and ordinances for the living. To my knowledge, this particular evidence of God’s truth, His universal love for the living and the dead, is not seen elsewhere in the world—except in one church that demonstrates truth in this particular regard: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. My first sight-giving, life-giving encounter with real evidence of truth did not come with anointing clay or in the pool of Siloam. No, the instrument of truth that brought my healing from the Lord came as pages in a book, yes, the Book of Mormon: Another Testament of Jesus Christ! The claims about this book have been attacked and dismissed by some unbelievers, the anger often matching the vitriol of those who told the healed man that he could *not possibly* have experienced what he knew he *had* experienced. It has been hurled at me that the means by which this book came to be were impractical, unbelievable, embarrassing, even unholy. Now, that is harsh language from anyone who presumes to know the means by which the book came to be, inasmuch as the only description given about those means is that it was translated “by the gift and power of God.”[10](#fn-10) That’s it. That’s all. In any case, the impact of the Book of Mormon in my life is no less miraculous than was the application of spit and dirt placed on the blind man’s eyes. It has been, for me, a rod of safety for my soul, a transcendent and penetrating light of revelation, an illumination of the path I must walk when mists of darkness come. And surely they have, and surely they will. And given the view it has granted me of my Savior’s universal love and redeeming grace, I share with you my witness, justified here as the newly blessed man’s parents said their son should be heard because he was “of age.”[11](#fn-11) Well, so am I. He was old enough to be taken seriously, they implied. Well, so am I. I am two months away from my 85th birthday. I have been at the edge of death and back. I have walked with kings and prophets, with presidents and apostles. Best of all, I have at times been overwhelmed by the Holy Spirit of God. I trust that my witness should be given at least some consideration here. Now, brothers and sisters, I came to my whole-souled conviction that The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints is a true restoration of the New Testament Church—and more—because I could not deny the evidence of that restoration. Since those first experiences, I suppose I have had a thousand—ten thousand?—*other* evidences that what I have spoken of today is true. So I am delighted now to join my friend huddled on the streets of Jerusalem, where with my diminished voice I sing: Amazing grace—how sweet the sound— That saved a wretch like me! I once was lost, but now am found, Was blind, but now I see.[12](#fn-12) In the name of Jesus Christ, amen. --- ## Footnotes **1.** See [John 9:1–7](../../../scriptures/nt/john/9.md). **2.** See [John 9:13–25](../../../scriptures/nt/john/9.md). **3.** [John 9:3](../../../scriptures/nt/john/9.md). **4.** See [John 9:6, 11](../../../scriptures/nt/john/9.md). **5.** [John 9:3](../../../scriptures/nt/john/9.md). **6.** See [2 Kings 5:1–15](../../../scriptures/ot/2-kgs/5.md). **7.** See [Numbers 21:4–9](../../../scriptures/ot/num/21.md); [1 Nephi 17:41](../../../scriptures/bofm/1-ne/17.md); [Alma 33:18–22](../../../scriptures/bofm/alma/33.md). **8.** [Isaiah 53:2](../../../scriptures/ot/isa/53.md). **9.** [Luke 9:1](../../../scriptures/nt/luke/9.md). **10.** [Omni 1:20](../../../scriptures/bofm/omni/1.md); see also [Doctrine and Covenants 20:35](../../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/20.md). **11.** [John 9:23](../../../scriptures/nt/john/9.md). **12.** “[Amazing Grace](../../../music/hymns-for-home-and-church/amazing-grace.md),” Hymns—For Home and Church, Gospel Library. Keep total output under 300 words. No other text.
